title: Electrical & Optical Signals
description: How binary data is represented as electrical voltages, light pulses, or radio waves on physical media.
fullExplanation: At the physical layer, bits (0s and 1s) must be converted into physical signals. Copper cables use voltage levels (e.g. +5V = 1, 0V = 0 in NRZ encoding). Fiber optic cables use light pulses (light on = 1, off = 0). Wireless uses radio wave modulation. The encoding scheme determines how bits map to signal states. More complex encodings (like 4B/5B, 8B/10B, PAM4) improve efficiency and clock recovery.

encodingSchemes.name: NRZ (Non-Return to Zero)
encodingSchemes.description: High voltage = 1, low = 0. Simple but no clock sync.

encodingSchemes.name: NRZI
encodingSchemes.description: Transition on 1, no transition on 0. Used in USB.

encodingSchemes.name: Manchester Encoding
encodingSchemes.description: Transition in middle of bit period. Self-clocking. Used in 10BASE-T.

encodingSchemes.name: 4B/5B
encodingSchemes.description: 4 data bits encoded as 5 bits — ensures enough transitions for clock recovery. Used in 100BASE-TX.

encodingSchemes.name: 8B/10B
encodingSchemes.description: 8 data bits → 10 bits. DC balance + clock recovery. Used in Gigabit Ethernet, Fibre Channel.

encodingSchemes.name: PAM4 (Pulse Amplitude Modulation 4)
encodingSchemes.description: 4 voltage levels encode 2 bits per symbol. Used in 25G/100G+ Ethernet.

keyFacts: Signal travels at ~2/3 speed of light in copper (~200,000 km/s)
Attenuation: signal weakens with distance — repeaters regenerate it
Noise: EMI, crosstalk, thermal noise degrade signal quality
SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio): higher = better signal quality
Bandwidth: maximum frequency range the medium can carry
Shannon's theorem: max data rate = B × log2(1 + SNR)
PAM4 doubles throughput vs NRZ at same symbol rate
